fact: May 9 is exactly 129 days into the year - that's like counting to 129 Mississippi's without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, dandelions are typically going wild right now, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes everywhere like nature's confetti cannons.
Fireflies often start blinking around this time as they warm up for summer's big light show - May 9 is prime firefly boot camp season!
The sun usually rises before 6 AM in most of North America by May 9, which means even sleepyheads get blasted with morning light.
Tree pollen typically reaches peak levels around May 9 in many northern regions - cars turn yellow-green like they're wearing moldy cheese coats.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere spots, May 9 marks the time when leaves often start their colorful autumn transformation, turning red and gold before dropping.
Tadpoles in ponds are usually sprouting their back legs right around now - imagine growing legs while swimming, like a weird underwater superhero transformation!
May 9 sits right in the middle of Taurus season, since the zodiac switches from stubborn Taurus to chatty Gemini around May 20.
Night crawlers and earthworms often emerge in huge numbers after May rain showers, making the soil look like spaghetti soup.
In northern regions, ice on lakes typically makes its final dramatic exit around this time, cracking and groaning like a giant's breakfast cereal.
May 9 falls during the time when baby birds are often at their ugliest - all bald heads, bulging eyes, and gaping mouths that look like tiny dinosaurs.
The evening sky around May 9 shows off spring constellations like Leo the Lion, who's roaring his way across the heavens after sunset.
